nuggets.headline: From school band 'On a Friday' to music industry innovators
nuggets.text: Before they were Radiohead, Thom Yorke, Colin Greenwood, Jonny Greenwood, Ed O’Brien, and Phil Selway started out as 'On a Friday' while still at Abingdon School in Oxfordshire. They were signed just out of college, right when the shoegaze and Britpop scenes were buzzing, but quickly made a name for themselves by always pushing rock music's boundaries and even their own creative limits. This drive to do things differently has been their hallmark, even allowing them to successfully release albums like _In Rainbows_ on their own terms.

source.title: A Guide to Radiohead’s Studio Albums, from 'Pablo Honey' to 'A Moon Shaped Pool'
source.quoteSnippet: Radiohead emerged from Abingdon School in Oxfordshire, England, originally called On a Friday. The band, consisting of Thom Yorke, Colin Greenwood, Jonny Greenwood, Ed O’Brien, and Phil Selway, was signed just out of college.

nuggets.imageCaption: Radiohead in their early career, embodying their unique approach.

nuggets.headline: This song builds its hypnotic drive with three distinct guitar layers
nuggets.text: Notice how the drums stay steady throughout this track? Radiohead creates all the forward motion in 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' through a brilliant layering technique, adding textures and intricate arpeggios that shimmer and build. Ed O’Brien kicks things off with the first arpeggio, establishing the main harmony, then Thom Yorke joins in with a complementary arpeggio, and finally, Jonny Greenwood adds a third layer, twisting and inverting the progression to form a richer, more complex sound. It's a journey about fighting life's currents, resisting temptations, and ultimately resurfacing stronger.

source.title: Harmonic Analysis of Radiohead’s “Weird Fishes/Arpeggi”
source.quoteSnippet: Radiohead creates momentum in 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' in a textural way, by increasing the density of the arrangement with the addition of several layers.

nuggets.imageCaption: Musical notation shows the intricate, layered arpeggios of 'Weird Fishes.'

nuggets.headline: If Thom Yorke's emotive voice hooks you, try Jeff Buckley's 'Grace'
nuggets.text: If the melancholic beauty and intricate guitar work of 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' is hitting just right, then you absolutely need to dive into Jeff Buckley. Like Thom Yorke, Buckley was celebrated for his powerful, often falsetto, vocals and his ability to craft songs that were both complex and deeply emotional. His most iconic album, _Grace_, shares that atmospheric, introspective, and beautifully layered quality that you're hearing right now with Radiohead.
nuggets.imageCaption: Jeff Buckley's soaring vocals and intricate music parallel Radiohead's artistry.

artistSummary: Radiohead, originally On a Friday from Oxfordshire, consistently redefined rock music by challenging conventions and their own artistic boundaries. They famously released their album _In Rainbows_ as a pay-what-you-want download, thriving by doing things differently in the music industry.
